title: Chapter 40: Ashes of Us
content: The city doesn't matter.

That's the thing I've learned, in the months since we left the cottage behind like a shed skin. The city doesn't matter. What matters is that we're in one — that we chose to be in one, that we walked into the noise and the crowds and the million indifferent strangers and said: this is where we live now.

Our office is on the third floor of a building that used to be a textile warehouse. The floors are reclaimed wood, scarred and imperfect, and the windows rattle when the trains pass. There's a conference table Heath built himself from salvaged timber and pipe fittings — it wobbles if you lean too hard to the left, which our clients never do because they're too busy trying not to cry.
Vance & Cruz Consulting. We help victims of institutional abuse find legal recourse.

That's what the business cards say. What they don't say is that we use the Linton flash drive like a scalpel — carving into the financial records, the buried complaints, the hushed settlements, the rot that festers beneath the marble floors of every academy, every foundation, every gilded institution that ever looked the other way. We don't burn them down. Not anymore. We hand the evidence to lawyers who are hungry enough and clean enough to use it. We connect the broken to the people who can make the broken whole — or at least less broken. Or at least heard.
The monster becoming the useful monster.
I like the taste of that. It sits on my tongue like black coffee — bitter, but it wakes me up.

This morning, I'm the first one awake. I usually am. The apartment is above the office, and the radiator clicks and groans like it's trying to remember a song it once knew. I stand in the kitchen in one of Heath's shirts — too big, smells like cedar and the sandalwood soap he buys from the shop on Green Street — and I drink my coffee black and I check the exits.
Back door, deadbolt engaged. Window over the sink, latched. Fire escape, clear.

I'll check again before bed. I'll check again when I wake up in the middle of the night with my heart kicking like something caged. I'll check again when Heath's breathing changes and I know he's dreaming of the blizzard, of the music room, of the sound his father's belt made against the wall.
We're still broken.
The broken pieces just fit together in a way neither of us expected.

Heath emerges from the bedroom at seven. He always counts the steps — fourteen from the bed to the bathroom, nine from the bathroom to the kitchen. He doesn't try to hide it anymore. I don't try to stop checking the exits. We gave up pretending we were anything other than what we are: two people whose damage is so specific, so precisely shaped, that the jagged edges interlock.
"Morning," he says, and his voice is rough with sleep, and I love the sound of it like I love the sound of rain on the warehouse roof — not because it's beautiful, but because it means I'm not alone.
"Morning."

He kisses the top of my head as he passes. His fingers trail across my shoulder — casual, absent, the way you touch something you know will be there. The way you touch something that belongs to you. That you belong to.

The mornings are quiet. We eat breakfast standing at the counter — toast with almond butter for him, nothing for me until he slides half of his onto a plate and pushes it across with a look that says *don't start.* The evenings are work — case files, strategy sessions, the slow and unglamorous labor of turning evidence into justice. The nights are the only time we stop being strategists and start being people who chose each other.
It's not romantic. It's not gentle. It's real, and real is harder.
The letter arrives on a Thursday.

I know it's from Julian before I open it — the handwriting is unmistakable, all sharp angles and architectural precision, even on an envelope. The stamp is French. The paper is cream-colored and expensive, because some things Julian Linton will never be able to let go of.
I sit at the conference table — the wobbly one, the one Heath built — and I read it.
*Cat,*

*I'm in Lyon. I'm studying architecture. Real architecture — the kind that holds people up instead of trapping them inside. I can't tell you why I chose it. Maybe because buildings don't lie. They either stand or they fall, and you always know which.*

*I'm okay. Not healed — I don't think that word means what I used to think it means. But okay. I eat breakfast. I sleep most nights. I walked along the Rhône at sunset last week and felt something I hadn't felt in a long time.*
*I didn't feel like I was watching someone else's life.*
*I found something to live for that isn't a target. I hope you have too.*
I read it twice.

The first time, I'm looking for the trap. The second time, I'm looking for the lie. There isn't one. There's just a boy who was raised inside a machine and walked out of it and is learning, slowly and painfully, how to be a person.
I don't write back. There's nothing to say that Julian doesn't already know — that we're both still standing, and that standing is enough.

But I take the letter to the shelf beside our bed, where the war notebook sits. It's not a war notebook anymore. The strategies are gone, the troop movements, the supply lines. Now it's a journal — rough and half-formed, the kind of thing I never thought I'd keep. I slip Julian's letter between the pages, next to the dried black rose that crumbles a little more each month and the photograph of the parallel lines carved into the conservatory planter.
The lines I carved when I was seventeen.
The lines that were my first language, before I learned to speak in fire.
That evening, I'm washing dishes.

Let me say that again, because the absurdity of it still catches me off guard: I, Catherine Vance, am washing dishes. By hand. In a sink that has a dripping faucet I keep meaning to fix. The water is hot and faintly metallic, and my hands are pruning, and there's a spot of tomato sauce on my wrist that I haven't bothered to wipe away, and I am doing something so monstrously ordinary that the girl I was at seventeen would have set the kitchen on fire rather than participate in it.
But that girl didn't survive. I did.

The dishsoap smells like lemon and something synthetic, chemically sweet in a way that clings to your fingers. The plates are white and chipped — mismatched, like everything in this apartment, like everything in our lives. I can hear the city outside: a siren, distant, threading through the traffic, a motorcycle revving three blocks away, someone's music bleeding through an open window. The radiator clicks. The faucet drips.
I'm rinsing a glass when I feel him behind me.

Heath doesn't announce himself. He never has. He moves like what he is — something that learned to be silent in order to survive. His arms slide around my waist, and I feel the warmth of him against my back, the solid weight of his chest, the steady drum of his heartbeat. He rests his chin on my shoulder, and his breath is warm against my neck, and he smells like the sandalwood soap and the cold night air from when he stepped out onto the fire escape to think.
Neither of us speaks.

In the window above the sink, the glass is dark enough to hold a reflection. I see us — two people, backlit by the kitchen light. No masks. No stage. No audience. Just a man with his arms around a woman at a kitchen sink, both of them still slightly startled by the ordinariness of it, by the fact that they get to have this — this small, unremarkable, incalculably precious thing.
Two monsters who found each other in the dark and decided that was enough.
I turn off the water. The faucet drips. The radiator clicks.

I could stay here forever. I won't. I know that the night will bring its terrors — his, mine, ours. I know I'll wake at 3 AM with my pulse in my throat and my eyes hunting the shadows. I know he'll count the steps to the bathroom in the dark, counting to prove he's somewhere real. I know that the broken doesn't mend, not really. It just learns to bear weight.
But right now, in this kitchen, with his arms around me and the dishes still dripping in the rack, I am not afraid.
That's not the same as being happy. But it's close enough to touch.
Heath shifts behind me. His hand finds mine — the left one, the one that's still damp from the dishwater. He lifts it gently, turns it palm up.
I know what he's looking at before I see it.
The pact scar.

It's faded now. Months of healing, of time, of the slow alchemy that turns wounds into something else entirely. It's silver where it used to be red, a thin raised line across my palm like a river on a map leading nowhere — or leading everywhere. I've stopped trying to hide it. There's no one left to hide it from.
He traces the scar with his thumb, slow and deliberate. The same way he did in the music room, a lifetime ago, when we were still figuring out what we were and whether it would destroy us.
The answer was yes. It did destroy us. And here we are anyway.
"Do you remember what we promised?" His voice is low, rough, close to my ear.
I close my eyes. I can still feel the blade, the bright sharp kiss of it, the way the blood welled up like an answer to a question we hadn't finished asking.
"To burn it all down."
"We did."
I open my eyes. In the window, our reflection watches us — patient, unhurried, two people at the end of something that was also a beginning.
"And what about after?"
Heath lifts my hand. He presses his lips to the scar — not a kiss of worship, not a kiss of possession. A kiss of recognition. The way you touch something you've earned.
"After is us."

The words land in my chest like a stone dropped into still water. Ripples spreading outward, touching every shore. After is us. After is this — the dishes and the dripping faucet and the rattling windows and the city noise and the broken sleep and the counted steps and the checked exits and the journal full of letters and dried flowers and photographs of parallel lines.
After is a consultancy that helps victims fight back.
After is a kitchen that smells like lemon soap.

After is two monsters in an apartment above a warehouse, choosing each other every single day — not because they have to, not because the game demands it, not because the strategy requires it, but because they want to.
Because after the burning, after the ash, after the smoke clears — you're left with whatever you can build.
And we chose to build.

Heath releases my hand, but he doesn't let go of me. I turn in his arms and face him — really face him, not the version of him I strategized around or performed for or hid from, but the actual man. The one with the scar above his eyebrow from his father's ring and the small tattoo on his wrist that I still don't know the story behind and the slight stoop in his shoulders from a lifetime of making himself smaller in rooms where louder men took up all the space.

He looks at me the way he's always looked at me — like I'm a problem he wants to solve and a question he wants to ask and a door he wants to open all at once. Like I'm dangerous. Like he doesn't care.

I look out the window behind him. The city lights are scattered across the darkness like spilled gems — millions of people living their lives, going home to their apartments, eating their dinners, fighting their quiet fights, unaware of the two monsters among them. Two monsters who once burned an institution to the ground, who played games with lives like they were chess pieces, who carved promises into their own skin and meant every drop of blood.
And now here we are. In the middle of all that ordinary life. Passing for human.

I think about the girl I was at seventeen — the one who sat in the conservatory with a piece of broken glass and carved parallel lines into stone. Two lines, never touching. That was what I believed love was. That was what I believed safety looked like: close but separate. Intimate but inviolate. Two lines running in the same direction, forever, without ever crossing.
I was wrong.

The lines crossed. The lines burned. The lines became something I never could have planned for — not a weapon, not a strategy, not a game. Just this. Two people who destroyed each other and put each other back together, piece by jagged piece, until the seams held.

I'm not that girl anymore. But I'm not different, either. I'm the same shape, just filled with something else. I'm still a monster. I still dream in strategies. I still calculate angles in every room I enter. I still know fourteen ways to destroy a man with the contents of a kitchen drawer.

But I also know how to wash dishes. How to keep a journal. How to put a letter next to a dried flower and a photograph and understand that those things are not weapons — they're memories. And memories, unlike weapons, don't need to draw blood to prove they're real.
I'm not healed. I'm not redeemed. The word *redemption* implies that I've become good, and I haven't. I've become useful. There's a difference, and it matters.
I'm more honest than I was. More broken than I was. More whole than I was.
I don't know how all of those things can be true at the same time. But they are.

I turn in Heath's arms. I take his face in my hands — I feel the roughness of his jaw, the warmth of his skin, the faint tremor of someone who is still surprised, after all this time, that he's allowed to be touched without pain.
I kiss him.

Not strategic. Not performative. Not the calculated kiss of a girl playing a game, nor the desperate kiss of two people running from death, nor the dramatic kiss of a climax. Just a kiss. Soft and slow and unhurried, tasting like the coffee he drank this morning and the cold air he brought in from the fire escape and something underneath that I can only describe as *home*, which is the most dangerous word in any language and the only one that matters.

He kisses me back. His hands settle at the small of my back, and he holds me like I'm something worth holding — not carefully, not reverently, but firmly. The way you hold something you have no intention of letting go of. The way you hold something that's yours.

Outside, the city hums its million private songs. The faucet drips. The radiator clicks its mechanical mantra. Somewhere, a siren wails and fades. Somewhere, a door opens and closes. Somewhere, victims of the institutions we're dismantling are falling asleep for the first time without fear.
We did that. Two monsters, building something from the ruins.

It won't last forever. Nothing does. The Linton drive will run out of secrets eventually. The law firms will grow cautious. The institutions will adapt, the way institutions always do — building new walls, digging new graves, finding new ways to look the other way. And when that happens, we'll find new ways to fight. Or we won't. Maybe we'll just be two people in a city, washing dishes and checking exits and counting steps.
Maybe that's enough.
Maybe that's everything.

I pull back from the kiss and look at him — really look, the way you can only look at someone when you've seen them at their worst and their best and their most broken and their most terrifying and you've decided that all of it, every jagged piece, belongs to you.
"We burned it all down," I say.
"We did." His thumb traces my jaw. "And?"
"And we're still here."

He smiles. It's not a nice smile — it never is, with Heath. It's the smile of a boy who survived a blizzard, who walked into fire for someone else's sake, who chose the monster over the saint and never once looked back.
"Still here," he agrees.

I lean my forehead against his. The city glitters through the window — indifferent, enormous, alive. We're two small things inside it. Two monsters who found each other in the dark and decided that finding was better than hunting. That building was better than burning. That *after* was a word worth defining.
We are the ashes of who we were.
And from ashes, you can build anything.
Even something worth keeping.
